planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 8–9 plant September–March. Beacon Silver spreads vigorously by surface runners and fills in shade areas quickly — space 12–15 inches apart for a continuous groundcover mat within one season. In zone 9 the plant may go summer-dormant during intense heat; reduce watering in summer and it will rebound in fall. Semi-evergreen in mild winters; provides year-round groundcover in zone 7–9.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Beacon Silver tolerates a wider range of soil conditions than most shade groundcovers; Beacon Silver is among the most effective and reliable shade groundcovers for dry to moist shade conditions, producing a dense, weed-suppressing mat of nearly entirely silver-white leaves with only a thin green margin remaining — the highly reflective silver foliage brightens dark shade areas and makes Lamium an effective lighting element in shaded landscape designs. In spring the mat is covered with delicate pink-lavender flowers that continue for several weeks, adding floral interest during the prime shade gardening season. Spotted dead nettle (Lamium maculatum) is a European woodland native that naturalizes readily in North American garden conditions — it spreads by runners but is not considered invasive in most regions. The combination of easy care, excellent shade performance, dense weed suppression, and ornamental silver foliage that illuminates dark corners makes Beacon Silver one of the most planted shade groundcovers in North America.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden — Lamium maculatum Beacon Silver
North Carolina State University Extension — Shade Groundcovers for the Southeast
Pennsylvania Horticultural Society — Lamium for Shade Gardens

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or early fall; in zone 8–9 plant September–March. In zone 9 Obsidian benefits from afternoon shade protection and consistent moisture during summer. Divide clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor as the crown can lift out of the soil over time — replant divisions slightly deeper than the original position. The nearly black foliage of Obsidian maintains better color in partial shade than in full sun, where high heat can fade the dark pigmentation.

directions: Plant in partial shade to light shade in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Obsidian is a Terra Nova Nurseries Heuchera cultivar producing some of the darkest foliage in the genus — rounded, slightly ruffled leaves of an intense, glossy, near-black purple that approaches true black in shade conditions, creating a dramatic contrast with lighter companion plants in shade garden designs. The deep coloration persists through the entire growing season without fading, which distinguishes Obsidian from many dark-leaved heucheras that lighten to bronze or reddish-purple in summer heat. The dark, satiny leaves create a striking design element when planted alongside silver-leaved plants (Lamium, Pulmonaria), chartreuse-leaved hostas, or bright-flowered shade perennials where the near-black color amplifies the visual contrast. In early summer, Obsidian produces tall, airy wands of small white flowers that hover above the dark foliage — a classic heuchera flowering display that attracts hummingbirds and beneficial insects. The evergreen to semi-evergreen habit provides year-round interest in zone 7–9 gardens.
sources: Terra Nova Nurseries — Heuchera Obsidian Cultivar Introduction
Missouri Botanical Garden — Heuchera Plant Finder
North Carolina State University Extension — Coral Bells for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows for good wind pollination — long single rows pollinate poorly, resulting in incomplete ear fill. In zone 9 plant in spring and again in August for a fall crop. Trucker's Favorite has a broader window between roasting-ear and field-corn stages than modern sweet corn — if roasting-ear harvest is missed, the larger, dough-stage ears can be used for grits and dried cornmeal.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with good air circulation; Trucker's Favorite is an open-pollinated Southern heirloom corn variety that was the standard large-eared roasting corn sold at Southern roadside stands, farmers markets, and farm stands for much of the 20th century — the 'trucker' in the name refers to produce truck farmers who grew it as their preferred selling variety because the large ears (8–10 inches, white to yellow-white kernels) made an impressive and reliably satisfying roasting ear crop. At the roasting-ear stage (about 20 days after silk), the white to cream-colored ears are tender, starchy-sweet, and deeply flavored with the old-fashioned, rich, full corn character that modern hybrid sweet corns bred for extreme sugar content often lack — a more complex, substantial corn flavor similar to mid-century American corn. As a dual-purpose variety, Trucker's Favorite dried to an excellent yellow cornmeal with full corn flavor prized for Southern cornbread, grits, and polenta.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Trucker's Favorite Heirloom Corn
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Southern Heirloom Corn Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heirloom Sweet and Field Corn

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September for a fall harvest. Plant in solid blocks of at least 4 rows for adequate wind pollination. Ambrosia is a sugary-enhanced (SE) bicolor variety — isolate from field corn and popcorn by at least 250 feet or 2 weeks of tasseling time to prevent starch dilution by cross-pollination. The sugary-enhanced trait gives better flavor retention after harvest than standard sweet corn.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Ambrosia is an All-America Selections award-winning bicolor sweet corn recognized in 1982 for its outstanding combination of sweetness, tenderness, and one of the best flavor profiles of any bicolor sweet corn variety. The sugary-enhanced (SE) genetics give Ambrosia a higher sugar content and better post-harvest sugar retention than traditional open-pollinated sweet corn — the ears remain sweet and tender for several days after harvest rather than converting sugars to starch rapidly as standard sweet corn does. The bicolor ears with alternating white and yellow kernels have been the classic American sweet corn appearance for backyard gardens since the 1980s, and Ambrosia set the standard for bicolor flavor that later varieties aimed to match. The slender, well-filled ears (7–8 inches) have a fine kernel texture, tender pericarp (no toughness in the kernel skin), and outstanding raw flavor for eating directly off the cob without cooking. Ambrosia remains one of the most widely grown and recommended bicolor sweet corn varieties for home gardens.
sources: All-America Selections — Ambrosia Sweet Corn 1982 Award
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Sweet Corn Variety Evaluation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sweet Corn for Florida Gardens

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. Fuchsia grows as a perennial woody shrub in zones 10–11 and as a season-long annual in zones 3–9. In zone 9 it often survives mild winters — cut back in early spring and the woody base regrows. Hanging baskets require daily watering in summer heat; afternoon shade prevents wilting. Spent flowers drop cleanly, so deadheading is not required for continued bloom.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in well-drained, moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil or high-quality potting mix in containers; Dark Eyes is one of the most recognizable and widely grown fuchsia cultivars, distinguished by its richly colored double flowers with vivid red-pink sepals spreading backward from a full, rounded, deep violet-purple corolla — a classic fuchsia color combination that is simultaneously jewel-toned and reminiscent of tropical birds in flight. The pendant flowers are borne continuously throughout the growing season on trailing to semi-upright stems that make Dark Eyes ideal for hanging baskets, window boxes, and elevated container positions where the downward-facing flowers are viewed at eye level or from below. Fuchsia flowers are among the most reliably shade-tolerant of all flowering annuals, performing in deep shade conditions where most flowering plants fail to bloom adequately. Hummingbirds are extremely attracted to fuchsia flowers and will visit frequently, making Dark Eyes a reliable hummingbird garden plant in any shaded position.
sources: American Fuchsia Society — Dark Eyes Fuchsia Registration
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fuchsia Production Guide
Oregon State University Extension — Fuchsia Varieties for Pacific Northwest
